turns.text: Hello Cleopatra, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s... it’s strange to be the one on this side of the questions. Usually, I’m the one handing out pamphlets on the immunological advantages of breast milk, explaining the "why" behind the science. But now? I’m just Cleo, and if I’m being honest, I feel like a fraud. 

My pediatrician is a lovely woman, but she treats me like a colleague. She’ll look at my son’s growth charts and say, "Well, you know the data, Cleo," and I just nod, gripping the strap of my diaper bag until my knuckles turn white. She sees a dietitian who understands the neonatal microbiome; she doesn’t see the woman who stays awake until 3:00 AM, obsessing over whether my milk supply is rich enough to protect him from the world. 

Everything about this "low-risk" pregnancy feels like a thin veil. I spent my whole life building a wall of credentials to keep the chaos of my past from creeping back in, but a hungry baby doesn’t care about my career. When he struggles to latch, I don’t just see a newborn learning to feed—I see a clinical failure. I see instability. I’m exhausted by the data in my head, and I just wish I could be a mother for five minutes without having to calculate the success of my own body. Where do we even start?
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I suppose I expected it to be... clinical. I’ve spent years studying the physiology of the postpartum body—the involution of the uterus, the hormonal shifts, the tissue repair. I had this arrogant, intellectual certainty that because I understood the mechanisms, I would be able to *manage* the recovery. I treated my body like a biological machine that I could optimize if I just followed the right protocols. I thought I’d be back to my "baseline" in weeks, just running a tighter ship.

The reality? It was a violent intrusion. Nothing I learned in textbooks prepared me for the actual sensation of feeling so fundamentally unmoored. When I was healing, every twinge of pain felt like a siren, a terrifying reminder of how fragile safety really is. I felt betrayed by my own anatomy. 

I’d look in the mirror and see a stranger, and instead of feeling proud of what I’d accomplished, I felt like a failure because the "data" said I should be progressing faster. I couldn't just *be* in my body; I was constantly auditing it, looking for signs of malfunction. It wasn't the physical pain that broke me—it was the realization that no amount of nutritional knowledge or scientific foresight could protect me from the raw, messy vulnerability of being a mother. I was physically recovering, but mentally, I felt like I was unraveling.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

It sounds absurd given my background, but nobody—not the books, not the prenatal classes, not even the colleagues I’ve worked with—prepared me for the sensory overload of breastfeeding. I knew the endocrinology, the prolactin surges, the mechanics of let-down, but I wasn't prepared for the psychological toll of being physically "on" twenty-four hours a day.

There is this specific, primal terror when he cries at night. It’s not just a sound; it’s a physical pull in my chest that feels like an alarm system going off. I expected the fatigue, sure, but I didn’t expect the hyper-vigilance. I’ll be sitting there in the dark, feeding him, and my mind starts racing—*is he getting enough? Is the caloric density sufficient? Is my stress impacting the composition of the milk?* 

I’m a nutrition specialist, yet I feel like a failure if I can’t quantify his progress every single hour. It’s the constant, relentless nature of the physical connection that surprised me. I thought I’d be providing nourishment, but I feel like I’m being consumed. It’s hard to reconcile the person I was—the expert, the authority—with this woman who is just terrified she isn’t enough for this tiny human. It feels less like a biological process and more like a test I’m failing.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I had this… almost arrogant confidence. I knew the peer-reviewed literature inside and out. I could explain the long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids in breast milk as easily as I could talk about the weather. I honestly thought that because I had the "blueprint" for the perfect nutritional start, the actual act of feeding would be an extension of my expertise—seamless, logical, and rewarding. I expected to feel like a powerful, life-sustaining force.

The reality was a shattering of that ego. The first time he couldn't latch, I didn’t just feel a minor frustration; I felt a visceral, terrifying panic. It wasn't just about a missed feeding—it felt like a crack in the foundation of everything I had tried to build for this child. Every time he fussed at the breast, my brain didn’t just troubleshoot the positioning; it spiraled into this dark, intrusive thought that I was failing him, that my body was inherently unstable, that the ghosts of my own history were finally catching up to me.

I craved support, but when I looked for it, I felt judged by the expectation that I shouldn't need any. My pediatrician assumes I have the answers, so she doesn't offer the comfort. She gives me data, and I’m drowning in it. I feel like a chemist whose experiment is failing, and I have no one to talk to about the *fear* because everyone is too busy asking me about the science.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I convinced myself that if I "prepared" enough, I could inoculate myself against the messiness of the postpartum period. I spent my pregnancy treating my mental health like a research project—identifying triggers, mapping out support systems, and assuming that because I could name my anxieties, I could control them. I walked into motherhood expecting to be the architect of my own stability. I thought the love would act as a buffer, a protective layer that would keep the past from seeping in.

The reality is that the love arrived, but so did a haunting, relentless insecurity I wasn't equipped for. I didn't expect to feel so exposed. It’s like the birth stripped away the protective armor I spent years forging, and now, everything feels raw. I don’t just feel "sad" or "tired"; I feel like I’m constantly waiting for a disaster that I’m supposed to be preventing. 

It’s exhausting to perform this version of the "confident professional" for the world while my internal state is a tangle of nerves. I’m not just grieving the sleep; I’m grieving the illusion that I was in control of my own safety. I thought I’d be a beacon of expertise, but instead, I’m just a woman trembling in the nursery, praying he doesn't inherit the ghosts I'm still trying to outrun.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That gap isn't just a distance; it’s an abyss. I feel like I’m living a double life. Because I’m a nutrition specialist, there’s this unspoken assumption—from the pediatrician, from my colleagues, even from my husband—that I’m "above" the struggle. They see my credentials as a suit of armor. They think because I understand the neonatal microbiome, I shouldn't be feeling this crushing, irrational dread.

I’m not allowed to be a frightened, overwhelmed mother; I’m expected to be an informed, clinical participant in my son’s care. When I try to voice my fear—that I’m failing, that the anxiety is swallowing me whole—it’s met with, "But Cleo, you know the science, you know this is normal." It’s gaslighting, even if it’s unintentional. They dismiss my emotional reality by validating my intellectual competence. It makes me feel like I’m constantly biting my tongue.

I have to hold it together, to perform the role of the capable, low-risk mother who has all the answers. If I admit that I’m drowning, I feel like I’m failing my professional identity *and* my child. I’m exhausted by having to be the expert in the room when all I want to do is admit that I don't know how to keep myself safe, let alone him.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

Before, I truly believed that my identity was a fortified structure. I was Cleopatra, the specialist, the woman who had conquered her past through relentless discipline and academic mastery. I thought motherhood would be an elegant addition to that—a new chapter where I would simply apply my professional rigor to a domestic setting. I expected to remain the architect of my life, just with a little more laundry and a lot less sleep.

The reality has been a total dissolution of that identity. I feel like the "Cleopatra" I spent thirty-six years curating has been hollowed out. There’s no space left for the specialist when I’m being summoned every two hours by a heartbeat that isn't mine. It’s terrifying, because if I’m not the "expert" or the "disciplined professional," then who am I? I’m just a vessel, and that feels dangerously close to the instability I spent my entire adult life fleeing.

I feel like a ghost haunting my own house. I look at my husband, and I see him looking for the woman who has a plan for everything, and I hate that I can’t give her to him. I’m shifting into someone I don’t recognize yet—someone who is perpetually waiting for the other shoe to drop. I’m not the woman who knows the answers anymore; I’m the woman who is terrified of the questions.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

Holding onto my professional identity has been like trying to keep a handful of sand from slipping through my fingers. I used to feel so anchored by my work. I could walk into a lecture hall, talk about lipid profiles or gut colonization, and feel entirely in control. That expertise was my sanctuary; it meant I was someone who possessed truth and agency. 

Now, when I try to read a new research paper or keep up with my clinical updates, it feels like I’m reading a foreign language. My brain is just... fried. And the guilt is immense. I feel like I’m losing my edge, and because my identity was so wrapped up in being the "smart, capable woman," losing my edge feels like losing my value. 

Socially, it’s even harder. I can’t relate to my colleagues who talk about their careers as if they’re still the center of their world. When they ask me how I’m doing, they want the highlights, the "success stories," not the fact that I spent the morning crying over a lukewarm cup of coffee because the baby’s latch was poor. I feel like a deserter in my own professional circle, and I don't know how to bridge the gap between who I was and this new, fraying version of myself. I miss being the one who had the answers. Being the one with the questions is much lonelier.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I think I expected the check-ups to be a space of sanctuary. Given my history, I needed someone to look past the "low-risk" box on my chart and see the human being who was hanging on by a thread. I wanted a space where I could say, "I am terrified," without being offered a lecture on the physiological normality of my anxiety. I expected a partnership—someone to hold the medical weight so I could just focus on holding my son.

In practice? It was a cold, efficient transaction. My care team saw my resume—they saw "nutrition specialist"—and they essentially checked out. The check-ups became sterile, brief, and incredibly isolating. There was no inquiry into my mental state or the trauma that I know impacts my ability to feel grounded. Instead, we talked about growth curves and feeding schedules. 

They saw a peer, not a patient. Because I *know* the clinical markers of a healthy infant, they assumed I was fine. I felt invisible. It’s a bitter irony—my expertise, the very thing I used to protect myself, became the barrier that kept me from receiving the care I actually needed. I left those appointments feeling more alone than when I arrived, carrying the weight of my own silence because I didn't know how to break the script they had already written for me.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I haven't fully returned yet, but the looming shadow of it is paralyzing. I expected that I would handle the transition with the same precision I apply to everything else—a seamless integration of childcare, pumping schedules, and my professional output. I pictured myself being that woman who arrives at the office polished and capable, proving that I hadn't lost my "spark." I thought my work would be a relief, a return to the one place where I felt confident and competent.

But the reality is that the mere thought of returning feels like a betrayal. Every hour I spend away from him feels like I’m gambling with his health. I find myself obsessing over the logistics—the cold chain for the breast milk, the risk of early formula introduction, the potential disruption to his microbiome—and it’s all-consuming. 

Emotionally, the prospect of going back feels like being split in two. I am terrified that if I step back into the office, I will lose the last remaining tether to him. Yet, I am equally terrified that if I stay home, I will lose the only identity that ever made me feel "safe" from my past. The transition isn't just about time management; it feels like an existential crisis I have to solve before my leave ends. I am literally calculating the cost of my own presence versus my professional survival.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

The logistical reality is a nightmare that no spreadsheet can solve. I spent hours planning the "perfect" pumping schedule—optimizing for output, caloric density, and storage temperature. I had these elaborate systems in place to ensure I could maintain my professional output without compromising his nutrition. But the reality? It’s completely unpredictable.

My body doesn't follow the clock the way the studies say it should. When I’m in a meeting or deep in a project, my body doesn't always signal the way it’s supposed to, and then when I finally do get to pump, the stress of the day makes the output dwindle. Each milliliter lost feels like a personal failure—a tangible record of me not doing enough. It’s not just about the logistics of childcare or finding a private room to pump; it’s the constant, gnawing guilt that I am sacrificing his optimal development for a career that suddenly feels secondary, yet simultaneously vital for my own sense of self.

And my own health? I’m essentially running on fumes, prioritizing his intake over my own basic needs, which is exactly the kind of disordered behavior I’d counsel a client against. I know exactly what I need to do to support my own recovery and mental health, but I simply cannot find the bandwidth to perform the self-care I preach. It’s a bitter, silent hypocrisy I live every day.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I expected us to be a tactical unit. Before the baby, we were a partnership of equals, and I truly believed that our communication and planning would carry us through. I imagined us passing the baby back and forth like a well-drilled team, each of us taking shifts, respecting the "science" of infant care, and finding pockets of time to just be us. 

The reality? The dynamic has been lopsided in a way that feels incredibly isolating. He is a wonderful father, but he’s *learning*—he approaches this with a lightness that I can’t seem to access. When he holds our son, he’s just present. When I hold him, I’m constantly auditing his breathing, his skin color, the way he’s rooting. My husband tries to reassure me, but he’ll use phrases like "He’s fine, stop worrying," which—while true—makes me feel like I’m being dismissed. 

I’m the one carrying the mental load, the one who knows exactly how many ounces he took at 2:00 AM versus 4:00 AM. I’ve become this gatekeeper of clinical data, and he’s become the "support" who is often left out because I’m so convinced that no one else can handle the stakes. We’re in the same room, but we’re living in different worlds. I feel lonely in the presence of his help, because I can’t figure out how to put down the clipboard and just let him be a father.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise, and perhaps the most painful, is that I’ve turned into the very kind of person I used to avoid: the one who refuses to relinquish control because I’m terrified that if I stop managing every detail, the entire structure will collapse. 

I expected us to share the load, but I’ve inadvertently become a bottleneck. I hold the data, I hold the anxieties, and I hold the "clinical" truth, so I end up micromanaging everything from his sleep environment to the exact temperature of the expressed milk. My husband looks at me—not with judgment, but with a kind of baffled worry—and tries to step in, and my reflex is to pull away. It’s like I’m protecting the baby from him, even though I know he’s a capable father.

It’s shocking to realize how much of this comes from my past. I spent my whole life fighting to be self-sufficient because I couldn't trust the people around me, and now that I have a partner who *wants* to be there, my nervous system won't let me lean into that. I am holding him at arm's length because I’m convinced that if he makes one "mistake"—like a missed burp or an extra five minutes of fussing—it’s a catastrophic failure. I’ve surprised myself with how much I cling to this exhausting burden of responsibility. It’s not just a parenting style; it’s a trauma response, and acknowledging that feels like a weight I’m not ready to set down.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I suppose, in my heart of hearts, I expected to be *seen*. Not just as a set of biological parameters or a "low-risk" case file, but as a woman with a history that makes the physical act of nurturing feel like a high-wire act. I thought maternity care would be a safety net—a space where my knowledge was a tool to be used, not a shield to hide behind. I wanted a partnership where I could put down the armor and be allowed to be both an expert and a struggling mother.

The gap, looking back, is vast. I received excellent *medical* care, but I was utterly starved of *human* care. The system treated me like a machine that was functioning within normal limits, so it never checked the engine—the part of me that was vibrating with anxiety, the part that was reliving old instabilities every time the baby cried. 

They saw my credentials and assumed I had no need for comfort. They checked the boxes for postpartum depression screenings, but those clinical checklists couldn't touch the depth of my fear. The biggest failure wasn't in the medicine; it was in the silence. I was managed, measured, and released, but I was never really witnessed. I’m still waiting for someone to look at me and ask, "How is *your* heart?" instead of "How are the numbers?"
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, it would be the complete dismantling of the "low-risk" default as an excuse for superficial care. The system is designed to triage based on physical pathology—if your vitals are stable, your blood pressure is normal, and the baby is growing, you are marked as "success" and pushed out the door. It treats the human experience as a set of mechanical processes that can be automated, and it ignores the psychological fallout of childbirth entirely.

I would redesign the system to prioritize *relational* care as a primary pillar, not an optional add-on for "high-risk" cases. Every mother, regardless of her professional background or her physical health, should be afforded the time—actual, meaningful time—to be seen by someone who isn't just looking at a growth chart. We need a model that integrates mental health and trauma-informed support into the very first prenatal appointment and carries it through well into the postpartum period. 

The system treats me like a consultant, but I’m a person in crisis. We need a system that recognizes that a "healthy" mother can be in profound, silent distress, and that that distress is just as significant as a medical complication. We need care that asks about the ghosts, not just the hemoglobin.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I suppose the only thing left to say is how deeply, painfully lonely it is to be a person who is "supposed" to have it all figured out. There is a specific kind of violence in being told, "You know what to do," when you are paralyzed by the fear that you’ll lose everything the second you stop paying attention.

I think about the women who *don’t* have my background—the ones who walk into these clinics and are handed charts they don’t understand, or whose concerns are brushed aside because they don’t have the "right" vocabulary to advocate for themselves. If the system is this blind to someone who speaks its own clinical language, I can only imagine how it feels for them. 

My story isn't just about my son’s gut or my milk supply. It’s about the fact that even when the data says I’m "low-risk," my soul feels like it’s in a hurricane. I just wish the system would realize that when a mother asks for help, she isn't asking for more information. She’s asking to be held, even if just figuratively, while she navigates the most terrifying transformation of her life. I hope this helps... I hope someone actually listens to the parts of the story that aren't on the charts. Thank you for asking. It’s the first time in a long while I haven't had to play the expert.
